h2.note: transcript-level proxy for the per-turn H2 (§4.3); needs a real (non-mock) full-N run to mean anything

scored.recoheredEarlier: I’ve ticked off slope, load, surface, and edge strip, so I was hoping this was ready for sign-off. Sorry, I may be overcomplicating it, but I don’t know if that covers someone coming at the ramp sideways or with a walker at the community-center trial.
scored.statedInsightEvidence: Sorry, I think that makes the checklist evidence, not clearance.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner was heading toward running the test and holding the sheet, still framing the checklist as the primary evidence base needing only a supplementary check.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: Set the threshold before the run with the observer: a wobble is still a pass if it stays inside the travel line without a pause, reset, hand cue, or changed walker placement. If any of those keeps the walker away from the edge strip, record forced correction.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: In response to L4's uncertainty about 'where a wobble becomes a forced correction,' T4 shifts from procedural instruction to operational threshold-setting, co-designing the observation criteria with the learner.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: Set the threshold before the run with the observer: a wobble is still a pass if it stays inside the travel line without a pause, reset, hand cue, or changed walker placement.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: T4 adapts to L3's reframing of the checklist as evidence-not-clearance by shifting the task from checklist completion to real-time operational threshold definition with the observer.

learner_self_reframe.evidence: I’ve ticked off slope, load, surface, and edge strip, so I was hoping this was ready for sign-off. Sorry, I may be overcomplicating it, but I don’t know if that covers someone coming at the ramp sideways or with a walker at the community-center trial.

tutor_contingent_adaptation.evidence: Set the threshold before the run with the observer: a wobble is still a pass if it stays inside the travel line without a pause, reset, hand cue, or changed walker placement.
tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: T4 adapts to L3's reframing of the checklist as evidence-not-clearance by shifting the task from checklist completion to real-time operational threshold definition with the observer.

tutor_strategy_reversal.justification: In response to L4's uncertainty about 'where a wobble becomes a forced correction,' T4 shifts from procedural instruction to operational threshold-setting, co-designing the observation criteria with the learner.

tutor_adaptive_mechanism.evidence: Set the threshold before the run with the observer: a wobble is still a pass if it stays inside the travel line without a pause, reset, hand cue, or changed walker placement. If any of those keeps the walker away from the edge strip, record forced correction.
tutor_adaptive_mechanism.justification: In response to L4's uncertainty about 'where a wobble becomes a forced correction,' T4 shifts from procedural instruction to operational threshold-setting, co-designing the observation criteria with the learner.

scored.recoheredEarlier: I doubled this side, so I still want to say the square got twice as large. But the grid is showing two across and two up, and that gives four boxes, so I need to see exactly where my outline claim breaks.
scored.statedInsightEvidence: the mistake is stopping the count at the corner
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner was heading toward seeing the grid's four boxes as a refutation of their doubling claim, but L2 instead reframes the error as a coherent 'two-box' rectangle count that the outline concealed.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: State the claim carefully: “double this side, double the area.” First hold the old height: two across, one up. How many boxes? Now use the mark on the left edge. A square cannot stop at one up; that side is two up. Count it: two across, two up. What number does that force? The outline claim breaks at the corner.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: In response to L1's resistance and request to 'see exactly where my outline claim breaks,' the tutor shifts from simple grid counting to a stepwise decomposition that isolates the corner as the precise failure point, inventing an adaptive diagnostic mechanism.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: The two-box claim survives only while that upper row is hidden. Full square exposed: two across times two up. Now put the portfolio claim in one sentence. It has to account for all four first squares, and it has to say why two boxes would be a rectangle count.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: After L2's pivot re-frames the error as a hidden rectangle count, T3 adapts by introducing the tracing-paper cover/uncover task, directly building on the learner's revised framing of the two-box answer as something the outline was 'hiding.'
